Once again, Quentin Tarantino has delivered a beautifully crafted epic adventure. This movie was amazing and enjoyable from start to finish. The story was incredibly gripping and entertaining, and the friendship that was built between Django and Dr. Schultz was one of the sweetest I'd ever seen. Leonardo DiCaprio played one of the most heinous characters I've ever witnessed in film, and his performance was absolutely breathtaking. I definitely recommend this for action and western fans!

And who don’t like doctor Schultz. The visuals and costumes were an amazing touch. The scene with the bags over there head is one of the funniest things ever!

Can't find a single soul that didn't enjoy this movie. Very well written and casted excellently. Tarantino's formula wins again. Samuel L Jackson playing the most despicable self-hating African American since Uncle Ruckus was a Gem, especially knowing he's the exact opposite in real life. The soundtrack was the icing on the cake for this action packed theatrical entertainment piece. The action scene using vocals from James Brown and Tupac remixed together was the perfect pairing. It's a must see.

Django Unchained delivers a sleek, captivating revenge tale packed with sharp wit, explosive action, and genuine emotional weight. Jamie Foxx anchors the film with quiet power, Christoph Waltz brings effortless charisma, and Leonardo DiCaprio turns in a brilliantly unsettling villain. Tarantino fuses style and substance into a film that’s bold, unforgettable, and full of character.
One of my favorite western movies. The acting will have you drawn and the plot will have you enthralled!

When I think of my favorite movies, this is one that always comes to mind. Love him or hate him, Quentin Tarantino makes some great revenge fantasy movies and this, in my opinion, is one of his best. Great cast, sound track, action, emotion, revenge. If you wanna see evil people lose, this is a great watch.
This along with Kill Bill best QT movies of all time imo 🔥🔥🔥

I’m giving it four stars because it has some great acting and a few excellent moments but in truth it’s closer to a 3.5. It’s stuffed with boring filler, runs far too long, and the ending is, in my opinion, pretty rubbish.

A deliciously crazy movie by a deliciously crazy director, carried by a deliciously crazy cast. The final act has a few clichés that keep it from being perfect, but it's almost there. Get in the saddle and watch this one, it's a hell of a ride. Tip: watch the 1966 "Django" movie with Franco Nero first, in order to enjoy his cameo and nods to the original. Or don't, it's still great.

My all time favorite movie, I have watched it more times than I can count on my fingers. The stars execute their roles masterfully, hats off to the talented Christoph Waltz! Beautiful set, costumes and filming techniques - we expect no less from Tarantino. 10/10

Django Unchained is unapologetically Tarantino — violent, provocative, and unflinchingly bold. Critics who balk at its frequent use of the “N” word, depictions of racial violence, or over-the-top gore should remember: this is not a children’s movie. It is a cinematic reckoning with slavery, filtered through Tarantino’s audacious lens, complete with stylized gunfights that pay homage to The Wild Bunch and other genre classics. For those unprepared for such intensity, perhaps a PG-13 John Wayne western is more suitable. For everyone else, this is a film for the big boys: darkly funny, viscerally thrilling, and a masterclass in audacious storytelling. Tarantino blends historical brutality with cinematic bravado, creating a film that is both morally complex and unforgettably entertaining.

I absolutely loved Django Unchained. It’s one of Tarantino’s best—bold, stylish, and unflinchingly confident in both its storytelling and tone. From the very first scene, it pulls you into a brutal, beautifully shot revenge story that’s equal parts thrilling, funny, and deeply satisfying. The blend of spaghetti western and southern gothic is handled masterfully, giving the film a unique personality that feels both classic and completely fresh. Jamie Foxx delivers a fantastic performance as Django, evolving from a quiet, broken slave into a determined and fearless hero. Christoph Waltz, as Dr. King Schultz, steals every scene he’s in—his charm and wit balancing out the film’s darker, bloodier moments. Leonardo DiCaprio is mesmerizing as the cruel and charismatic Calvin Candie, bringing a twisted energy that’s impossible to look away from. Even Samuel L. Jackson’s role as the scheming house slave adds a surprising and unsettling depth to the story. The dialogue is sharp, the pacing deliberate but never dull, and the violence—while intense—is used to full dramatic effect. Every gunfight, every confrontation feels earned. The soundtrack is another standout, mixing Ennio Morricone-style western themes with modern tracks that somehow fit perfectly. What really makes Django Unchained work, though, is its sense of justice. Beneath all the style and blood, it’s a story about revenge, freedom, and love. It’s brutal and at times uncomfortable, but it never loses its emotional core. Tarantino turns a revenge fantasy into something both epic and deeply personal, and by the end, it feels cathartic in the best way. In short, Django Unchained is violent, funny, gorgeous, and unforgettable—a wild, unfiltered western with real heart and purpose.
One of the best films I’ve ever seen.
Quentin Tarantino doesn't shy away from the horrors of slavery in Django Unchained even as he delivers a weird, wild, and bloody violent crowd-pleaser in this raucous salute to the spaghetti western.

SPOILER ALERT It goes without saying in any Quentin Tarantino film, but the writing in Django Unchained is second to none. The cinematography is just marvelous in this picture. I love the use of the quick zoom camera work. The acting performances are all extraordinary. The performance of Don Johnson as Big Daddy is absolutely iconic. Calvin Candie is, by far, my favorite Leonardo Dicaprio performance. It's obviously the least like Leo of any character he's ever played. He had his reservations in playing such a diabolical character, but he executed the assignment with perfection. Stephen is one of my all-time favorite Samuel L. Jackson roles as well. Christoph Waltz is not only a linguistic genius, but a genius on the screen. He won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actor for his efforts. My only issue with that is that Leonardo Dicaprio should have been nominated for his extraordinary performance, as well as Samuel L. Jackson. Jonah Hill, no matter how small the role, was a riot. Quentin Tarantino's cameo as one of the Klansmen is hilarious. For years, I had no idea that was even him. He was also brilliant as the Australian slaver. I know some complain about his accent, but I have no issue with it. Every Australian person doesn't speak exactly alike. So that's just the way the guy spoke. Problem solved. This motion picture has masterpiece written all over it. The original song, "Ancora Qui" is so beautiful. It was composed by the legendary Ennio Morricone and performed by Elisa. The entire score of Django Unchained is glorious. The only song that I'm not a fan of, in fact, I actually hate it, is "100 Black Coffins". It just doesn't fit the film in my humble opinion. If Quentin were going to use a hip hop song, why not something from RZA, who made the song "Ode to Django" which ended up in the credits? I love that song, by the way. Rick Ross and his song just sound so generic to me. I guess part of it is that I view Ross himself as a phony to begin with. I do honestly hate the song, especially in this great film. The only other flaw that I find in Django Unchained is not an unforgivable one. It's when Calvin Candie is shot to death by Dr. King Schultz. The reaction time of Candie's men, specifically Butch, is quite delayed. My explanation for that, as someone who has been around a similar situation, is that time has a funny way of slowing down with the deafening sound of gunfire, especially if it resulted in someone getting shot. However, logically speaking, Butch's reaction time should have been faster due to the loud sound coming from behind him. Again, this isn't unpardonable, but it is worth mentioning. Other minor continuity errors, levels of liquid in drinks not matching and such, are really not worth mentioning. When Django slams the guy and his horse on the ground and then the horse is standing upright again in the very next shot is worth mentioning, but I honestly never noticed it until it was pointed out to me. Also, the cage at the end with the three slaves is open in one shot, closed in the next and then open again. I don't really consider these minor issues worthy of taking points off a film so masterfully done. I saw this legendary motion picture seven times in the theater, and countless times since in the comfort of my own home. If my memory serves me correctly, I purchased my copy of Django Unchained the very first day of its physical media release. To say that I love Django Unchained would be an understatement. This film is a must-watch for any true cinefile. 99/100
Amazing movie, with incredible acting especially Leo

This movie is a terrific love letter to Spaghetti Westerns. It reminds people exactly what made those films so special by incorporating some classic stylings mixed with modern sound editing and pacing. The opening credits, the songs sung about the main characters, the over-the-top squibs and even Django himself are all pitch perfect homages to the specific genre. It feels wrong to talk about the actors’ performances in this film, because I don't see the actors, I see the characters, that's how well everyone embodies their roles. Along with the visceral action, be prepared for some of the funniest scenes ever. The humor is not to the detriment to the action, it is to the overall enjoyment of the film. I won't spoil the jokes here, but they are comedy gold. My one problem comes towards the end. The movie has a detour late in the runtime that kills the momentum. The movie could have cut out those 15 minutes and the story would have been better off for it. But fittingly, that sort of detour does feel true to spirit to old Spaghetti Westerns.
